policy_statement: NAP target: target population of awareness/dissemination campaigns on climate impacts and adaptation measures to reach 5% by 2020 and 25% by 2030.
mechanism_description: Increase the reach of public awareness and dissemination campaigns about climate impacts and adaptation measures to support greater adaptive capacity.
primary_causal_pathway: Information/behavior change → ↑ preparedness & adaptive actions → ↓ vulnerability/exposure to climate hazards → ↓ climate-related damages
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the human system by expanding public communication, education, and outreach on climate impacts and practical adaptation options (e.g., what risks exist, what actions to take, when to act). (2) The mediating process is improved risk perception, knowledge, social norms, and decision-making that increase uptake of household/community preparedness and protective measures (and sometimes earlier response during events). (3) The climate-relevant outcome is higher adaptive capacity leading to reduced sensitivity and/or exposure and therefore lower losses from heat, floods, droughts, wildfire smoke, storms, and related climate hazards.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing climate-risk impacts by increasing preparedness and adoption of protective actions through improved public awareness and information.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: information dissemination increases preparedness and adaptive behavior, which reduces vulnerability/exposure to climate hazards and associated damages.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: the principal outcome is risk reduction (capacity/preparedness), not emissions reduction (Mitigation), not primarily “same service with less resource input” (Resource Efficiency), and not ecosystem-mediated benefits (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: awareness campaigns can incidentally reduce emissions if they promote energy-saving behaviors, but in this mechanism definition the content is explicitly climate impacts and adaptation measures, so the dominant pathway remains adaptation capacity building.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy fitting this mechanism—expanding adaptation-focused public awareness/dissemination—has its main climate effect through improved adaptive capacity and reduced hazard impacts.  
5) Note on subtypes: any finer secondary tagging (e.g., heat-health, flood preparedness, drought response, vulnerable-population outreach) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to the policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “awareness” content shifts toward mitigation behaviors (energy conservation, low-carbon transport) or is paired with strong efficiency programs, the dominant pathway could become Mitigation/Resource Efficiency; however, as defined here (adaptation measures), classification as Adaptation is straightforward.
key_indicators: “Target population”; “awareness or dissemination campaigns”; “related to climate change impacts and adaptation measures”; “5%”; “25%”; “by 2020”; “by 2030”
co_benefits: Improved general disaster preparedness and risk literacy; potential public health benefits (e.g., heat-health awareness); strengthened community resilience and emergency readiness
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an information-disclosure/communication measure, but the presence of explicit numeric coverage levels and time horizons makes “target-setting” the best fit.
